The **Prix de l'Arc de Triomphe** (French pronunciation: [[pʁi də laʁk də tʁijɔ̃f]](/wiki/Help:IPA/French "Help:IPA/French")) is a [Group 1](/wiki/Group_races "Group races") [flat](/wiki/Flat_racing "Flat racing") [horse race](/wiki/Horse_racing "Horse racing") in France open to [thoroughbreds](/wiki/Thoroughbred "Thoroughbred") aged three years or older. It is run at [Longchamp Racecourse](/wiki/Longchamp_Racecourse "Longchamp Racecourse") in [Paris](/wiki/Paris "Paris") over a distance of 2,400 metres (1 mile 4 furlongs). The race is scheduled to take place each year in the autumn, usually on the first Sunday in October.

With prize money of €5 million, the Prix de l'Arc de Triomphe, often referred to as the Arc, is Europe's richest flat race. The prize money and prestige of the race attract entries and spectators from across the world.[[2]](#cite_note-Post-2) Since 2008 the race has been sponsored by [Qatar](/wiki/Qatar "Qatar").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Prix_de_l%27Arc_de_Triomphe&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### Origins

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Prix_de_l%27Arc_de_Triomphe&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Origins")]

The Société d'Encouragement, a former governing body of French racing, had initially restricted its races to thoroughbreds born and bred in France. In 1863, it launched the [Grand Prix de Paris](/wiki/Grand_Prix_de_Paris "Grand Prix de Paris"), an event designed to bring together the best three-year-olds from any country. Thirty years later, it introduced the [Prix du Conseil Municipal](/wiki/Prix_du_Conseil_de_Paris "Prix du Conseil de Paris"), an international race for the leading horses of different age groups. It was run over 2,400 metres in October, with weights determined by a horse's previous performances.[[3]](#cite_note-Galop-3)

The creation of a third such race was proposed at a committee meeting on 24 January 1920. The new event would complement the Grand Prix de Paris and serve as a showcase for French thoroughbred breeding. It would have similar characteristics to the Prix du Conseil Municipal, but each horse would compete on equal terms, unpenalised for previous victories.[[3]](#cite_note-Galop-3)

Coming in the wake of World War I, it was decided that the race would be named after the [Arc de Triomphe](/wiki/Arc_de_Triomphe "Arc de Triomphe"), a famous monument which had been the scene of a victory parade by the [Allies](/wiki/Allies_of_World_War_I "Allies of World War I") in 1919. The chosen title had been previously assigned to a minor event at Longchamp. Another suggested title was the "Prix de la Victoire".[[3]](#cite_note-Galop-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4)

### Race history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Prix_de_l%27Arc_de_Triomphe&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Race history")]

The Prix de l'Arc de Triomphe was first run on Sunday, 3 October 1920. The inaugural running was won by [Comrade](/wiki/Comrade_(horse) "Comrade (horse)"), a three-year-old colt owned by [Evremond de Saint-Alary](/wiki/Evremond_de_Saint-Alary "Evremond de Saint-Alary").[[5]](#cite_note-5) The winner's prize was 150,000 francs.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

In 1935, the event secured state funding by the means of a [Sweepstake](/wiki/Sweepstake "Sweepstake"). The system was first used in 1936, and it continued until 1938.[[3]](#cite_note-Galop-3) The race was cancelled twice during World War II, in 1939 and 1940. It was run at [Le Tremblay](/wiki/Tremblay_Park "Tremblay Park") with a distance of 2,300 metres in 1943 and 1944.[[3]](#cite_note-Galop-3)

Government funding of the race resumed in 1949, with money obtained through the Loterie Nationale. Offering a jackpot of 50 million francs, this enabled a rapid increase of the prizes for both the Arc and its supporting races. By the 1970s, however, the assistance of the lottery had diminished, and the system was finally discontinued after the 1982 running. Since then, the race has had several sponsors, including [Trusthouse Forte](/wiki/Forte_Group "Forte Group"), CIGA Hotels and [Groupe Lucien Barrière](/wiki/Groupe_Lucien_Barri%C3%A8re "Groupe Lucien Barrière").[[3]](#cite_note-Galop-3)

Since 2008, the race has been sponsored by Qatar. The Arc weekend includes seven races classed at Group 1 level, and four with Group 2 status. It also features the [Arabian World Cup](/wiki/Arabian_World_Cup "Arabian World Cup"), the world's richest race for purebred [Arabian](/wiki/Arabian_horse "Arabian horse") horses, with €1 million in prize money.[[3]](#cite_note-Galop-3)

Due to renovations at the [Hippodrome de Longchamp](/wiki/Longchamp_Racecourse "Longchamp Racecourse"), the 2016 and 2017 editions of the race took place at [Chantilly](/wiki/Chantilly_Racecourse "Chantilly Racecourse").[[3]](#cite_note-Galop-3)

[**a**](#ref_1925a) Cadum finished first in 1925, but he was relegated to second place for hampering Priori.
  
[**b**](#ref_1943b) [**c**](#ref_1944c) The 1943 and 1944 editions were run at [Le Tremblay](/wiki/Tremblay_Park "Tremblay Park") over 2,300 metres.
  
[**d**](#ref_1959d) Midnight Sun dead-heated for first in 1959, but he was placed second for hampering Saint Crespin.
  
[**e**](#ref_1985e) Sagace finished first in 1985, but he was demoted to second for bumping Rainbow Quest.
  
[**f**](#ref_2016f) The 2016 and 2017 runnings took place at [Chantilly](/wiki/Chantilly_Racecourse "Chantilly Racecourse") while Longchamp was closed for redevelopment.
